title: [Bug]: kube generate ignores --read-only-tmpfs=
body:
### Issue Description

I run my container once with

> /usr/local/bin/podman run --cap-add=IPC_LOCK --log-driver=none --net=host --read-only --read-only-tmpfs=false --mount type=bind,src=/etc/k,dst=/conf --rm=true -a=stderr -a=stdout -a=stdin --userns=auto:size=10 --name=k --tty localhost/k:2023-01-25

and once with

> /usr/local/bin/podman run --cap-add=IPC_LOCK --log-driver=none --net=host --read-only --read-only-tmpfs=true --mount type=bind,src=/etc/k,dst=/conf --rm=true -a=stderr -a=stdout -a=stdin --userns=auto:size=10 --name=k --tty localhost/k:2023-01-25

The difference is the value of `--read-only-tmpf`.  After each run, I call `podman generate kube k` and compate the outputs of both `podman generate kube` invokations.  The only difference is metadata.creationTimestamp.

Therefore the option `--read-only-tmpf=true|false` is ignored by `podman generate kube`.

### Steps to reproduce the issue

see above

### Describe the results you received

see above

### Describe the results you expected

When the value of --read-only-tmpfs= changes, the output of `podman kube generate` should also change.

### podman info output

```yaml
APIVersion: 4.4.1-dev
  Built: 1675520042
  BuiltTime: Sat Feb  4 14:14:02 2023
  GitCommit: 9c5b249e126e8d999be8b2c342057bc3e3f5640b
  GoVersion: go1.19.3
  Os: linux
  OsArch: linux/amd64
  Version: 4.4.1-dev
```


### Podman in a container

No

### Privileged Or Rootless

Privileged

### Upstream Latest Release

Yes

### Additional environment details

Additional environment details

### Additional information

Additional information like issue happens only occasionally or issue happens with a particular architecture or on a particular setting

comments.body: A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
Does kubernetes.yaml support the concept of a readonlytmpfs? Or do you expect volumes to be specified with readonly mounts?

I do not know whether the pod/container definitions allow this.

As I do not need /tmp, I expect that the system does not allocate resources for /tmp .

> As I do not need /tmp, I expect that the system does not allocate resources for /tmp .

Fair enough, but `--read-only-tmpfs` affects other mounts too and for what it's worth if it's not supported by Kubernetes(haven't tested if it's possible to set emptyDir to read-only), then I _doubt_ it'll be supported here.

Also, afaik, there's no way to specify tmpfs-mounts as read-only in Kubernetes as a standalone flag/attribute, you can either use `securityContext.readOnlyRootFilesystem`(not ideal) or mount individual volumes and set them to read-only.

`--read-only-tmpfs=false` mounts the directories /tmp, /run and /var/tmp readonly.  For me there is  no difference between not having these directories, and having them empty and read-only.  To save resources, I would like not to have these directories, as I do not need them for some of my use cases.

`podman generate kube` already sets `spec.containers.readOnlyRootFileSystem: true`.  I do not know, whether this implies read-only /tmp, /run and /var/tmpfs .  In case it implies readonly /tmp, /run and /var/tmpfs and in podman the latter have to be read-write, then `podman kube generate` shall ensure these are read-write.

> podman generate kube already sets spec.containers.readOnlyRootFileSystem: true. I do not know, whether this implies read-only /tmp, /run and /var/tmpfs . In case it implies readonly /tmp, /run and /var/tmpfs and in podman the latter have to be read-write, then podman kube generate shall ensure these are read-write.

That flag sets the entire rootfs for the container as read-only, not just specific mounts. So unless or until you have an explicit non-read-only mount for your application to use, your workloads would suffer.

> --read-only-tmpfs=false mounts the directories /tmp, /run and /var/tmp readonly. For me there is no difference between not having these directories, and having them empty and read-only. To save resources, I would like not to have these directories, as I do not need them for some of my use cases.

That's completely reasonable and that's what the flag does but to extend it to kube-play in the manner discussed would be misleading for users of `podman kube ...`.  

Mounting individual tmpfs volumes as explicit mounts in the generated k8s manifest is the only way to go about it from what I can see, I don't know what problems that would cause because as a user, I'd certainly not expect a bunch of volume mounts in my k8s manifest which _migh_ have adverse affects If I take it over to a cluster to run. It'd have been a totally different argument if k8s had an attribute called `readOnlyTmpfs` in the pod spec in which case, something like this would've been trivial. What do you think?

The original problem description is, that the output of `podman kube generate` does not change, when `--read-only-tmpfs=true/false` is toggled., and `--read-only` is kept on `podman run`.  Since a month has passed, when I filled this trouble, I do not remember all the details.

In particular, I do not know whether the currently generated YAML implies read-only /tmp, /run/ and /var/tmp, or the latter are read-write.

I think that the YAML output shall change, when `--read-only-tmpfs=…` is altered and I am confident that you can find a good approach here.  For personal reasons I am short in time and I thus cannot investigate the specifics of Kubernetes to have a strong opinion here.
